nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich reefs and rocky areas in clear, well-lit shallow water.
nativeRange.biotope: Red Sea / Arabian Sea coral reef

diet.notes: Primarily grazes filamentous and macroalgae in the wild. In captivity, offer frequent feedings of marine-based seaweed (nori sheets clipped in the tank), spirulina, and vegetable-based prepared foods at least 2-3 times daily. A vitamin-rich, algae-heavy diet is generally considered important for reducing aggression and supporting immunity; specimens kept on nutritionally poor diets are more susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE).

goodWith: most peaceful reef fish of different body shape
avoidWith: small timid fish that can be bullied

imageCandidates.notes: Kora27, 3927x2602. Striking sharp full-body profile of a vividly coloured adult: deep purple body with fine dot/line facial pattern, bright yellow caudal fin and yellow pectoral base clearly shown against blue water.
Gdiggers, 2945x1852. Sharp clean aquarium profile facing the opposite direction; classic Zebrasoma disc shape, purple body with speckling and bright yellow tail all in frame.
Diego Delso, 4085x2723. Vibrant in-situ Red Sea individual; excellent detail on the yellow-and-purple pectoral fin and dotted face, deep purple body with yellow/orange tail.

summary: The Purple Tang is a Red Sea endemic surgeonfish recognized by its deep purple body and bright yellow tail. Widely considered more aggressive than most other members of its genus, it is generally kept singly in a large reef aquarium with plenty of open swimming space.
careNotes: The Purple Tang is a Red Sea endemic that also occurs in the Arabian Sea, Gulf of Aden, Persian Gulf, and Maldives, where it grazes filamentous algae over coral and rocky reefs at 0-20 m. Tanks of around 500 L (130+ US gal) with a long footprint are generally advised to give them the open swimming space and grazing surface they require. Like all surgeonfishes, they carry a sharp, scalpel-like spine on each side of the caudal peduncle (the narrow base of the tail) that can injure both handlers and tankmates, so caution is advised when netting. Purple Tangs are widely considered more aggressive than most other Zebrasoma; other tangs and similarly shaped surgeonfish should generally be avoided as tankmates unless the aquarium is very large and the tangs are introduced simultaneously. The species is widely considered reef-safe and is not known to bother corals or invertebrates. As high-metabolism grazers, they should have continuous access to algae (clipped nori, dried seaweed, macroalgae) and can decline rapidly without adequate plant matter; an insufficient vitamin- and algae-rich diet combined with poor water quality predisposes them to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and sensory line running down the body.
breedingNotes: Not commercially bred in captivity. Spawning occurs in the wild as pelagic broadcast events; rearing the planktonic larvae through metamorphosis has not been reliably achieved in the home aquarium.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product care sheet: 130 gal min, 72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, semi-aggressive (very aggressive toward other Zebrasoma), reef safe, herbivore, ~10 in adult.
